sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Your app is in us-east-1 (Virginia, USA).
A user in Mumbai loads your homepage with a 2MB hero image.

WITHOUT CLOUDFRONT:
User in Mumbai → internet → your EC2 in Virginia (15,000 km away)
Round-trip time: ~180ms per request
Loading the image: 180ms × many round trips = slow!
Plus: data transfer OUT from EC2 = $0.09/GB

WITH CLOUDFRONT:
First request: Mumbai user → CloudFront Mumbai edge → cache miss
→ CloudFront fetches from Virginia (once) → caches at Mumbai edge
Subsequent requests: Mumbai user → CloudFront Mumbai edge → cache hit
→ Served from Mumbai (< 5ms, 10km away instead of 15,000km)
→ Data transfer: $0.0085/GB (90% cheaper than direct EC2!)
→ Your EC2 barely gets any traffic (cached at edge)

CloudFront = global network of caching servers sitting between your users
             and your origin (S3, ALB, EC2, API Gateway, custom origin)
```

---

blocks.content:
Your app is in us-east-1 (Virginia, USA).
A user in Mumbai loads your homepage with a 2MB hero image.

WITHOUT CLOUDFRONT:
User in Mumbai → internet → your EC2 in Virginia (15,000 km away)
Round-trip time: ~180ms per request
Loading the image: 180ms × many round trips = slow!
Plus: data transfer OUT from EC2 = $0.09/GB

WITH CLOUDFRONT:

→ CloudFront fetches from Virginia (once) → caches at Mumbai edge
Subsequent requests: Mumbai user → CloudFront Mumbai edge → cache hit
→ Served from Mumbai (< 5ms, 10km away instead of 15,000km)
→ Data transfer: $0.0085/GB (90% cheaper than direct EC2!)
→ Your EC2 barely gets any traffic (cached at edge)

CloudFront = global network of caching servers sitting between your users
             and your origin (S3, ALB, EC2, API Gateway, custom origin)

sections.title: ⚙️ CloudFront Components
sections.rawMarkdown:
### CloudFront Big Picture Diagram
```
User
 │
 ▼
Nearest edge location
 │
 ├────────► cache hit → serve immediately
 │
 └────────► cache miss
              │
              ▼
           origin
   (S3 / ALB / API Gateway)
              │
              ▼
      object cached at edge

MENTAL MODEL:
CloudFront sits between users and origin, turning long-distance fetches into local cache hits.
```

```
DISTRIBUTION:
→ Your CloudFront configuration
→ Has a domain: d1234567890abc.cloudfront.net
→ Use Route 53 Alias to point your domain to this
→ Types: Web (HTTP/S) — use this for everything

ORIGIN:
→ Where CloudFront fetches content FROM when cache miss
→ S3 bucket (static files, website)
→ ALB (dynamic app behind load balancer)
→ EC2 instance (direct)
→ API Gateway endpoint
→ Any HTTP server (on-premise, other cloud)

EDGE LOCATIONS:
→ 400+ locations in 90+ cities worldwide
→ Where content is cached
→ Much more locations than AWS regions (30 regions vs 400+ edges)

REGIONAL EDGE CACHES:
→ Larger caches between edge locations and origin
→ If edge location cache expires → checks regional cache first
→ Less-popular content cached longer here
→ Reduces origin load further

CACHE BEHAVIOR:
→ Rules for how to cache specific URL patterns
→ /static/* → cache for 1 year (images, CSS, JS)
→ /api/*    → don't cache (or short TTL)
→ /         → cache for 1 minute
→ Each behavior can have different: TTL, allowed methods, forwarding rules
```

---

→ Where content is cached

REGIONAL EDGE CACHES:
→ Larger caches between edge locations and origin
→ If edge location cache expires → checks regional cache first
→ Less-popular content cached longer here
→ Reduces origin load further

CACHE BEHAVIOR:
→ Rules for how to cache specific URL patterns
→ /static/* → cache for 1 year (images, CSS, JS)
→ /api/*    → don't cache (or short TTL)
→ /         → cache for 1 minute
→ Each behavior can have different: TTL, allowed methods, forwarding rules

revisionPoints: → Your CloudFront configuration
→ Has a domain: d1234567890abc.cloudfront.net
→ Use Route 53 Alias to point your domain to this
→ Types: Web (HTTP/S) — use this for everything
→ Where CloudFront fetches content FROM when cache miss
→ S3 bucket (static files, website)

→ EC2 instance (direct)

sections.title: 🔒 CloudFront Security
sections.rawMarkdown:
### OAC — Origin Access Control (for S3)
```
PROBLEM:
S3 bucket with private files.
You want ONLY CloudFront to access S3, not users going directly to S3 URL.

SOLUTION: Origin Access Control
→ CloudFront signs requests to S3 with a special signature
→ S3 bucket policy: only allow requests signed by CloudFront OAC
→ Direct S3 access → denied (no valid OAC signature)
→ Via CloudFront → allowed (has valid signature)

BUCKET POLICY FOR OAC:
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Principal": {
    "Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"
  },
  "Action": "s3:GetObject",
  "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*",
  "Condition": {
    "StringEquals": {
      "AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:cloudfront::123456:distribution/EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE"
    }
  }
}

→ Block Public Access ON for S3 bucket (good!)
→ CloudFront OAC authenticates → gets objects
→ Users see content via CloudFront, never touch S3 directly
```

### WAF + CloudFront
```
Attach WAF (Web Application Firewall) to CloudFront:
→ WAF rules run at the EDGE (400+ locations)
→ Malicious traffic blocked before reaching your origin
→ SQL injection, XSS, rate limiting, IP blocking — all at edge
→ CHEAPER: blocked requests don't consume your origin capacity

COMMON WAF RULES ON CLOUDFRONT:
→ AWS Managed Rules (OWASP Top 10 protection)
→ Rate limiting: >1000 requests/5min from same IP → block
→ Geo-blocking: block specific countries
→ IP reputation lists (known bad IPs from threat intelligence)
```

### HTTPS + Custom Certificates
```
CloudFront forces HTTPS with custom domain:
1. Request SSL certificate in ACM (us-east-1 region ONLY for CloudFront!)
2. Attach certificate to CloudFront distribution
3. Configure: redirect HTTP → HTTPS
4. Route 53 Alias: your domain → CloudFront distribution

SECURITY POLICIES:
TLSv1.2_2021 (recommended): TLS 1.2+ only, modern cipher suites
TLSv1 (legacy): if you must support very old clients

SNI (Server Name Indication): FREE (CloudFront uses SNI for certificate selection)
Dedicated IP: $600/month (for very old clients that don't support SNI)
→ Use SNI unless you have specific reason not to
```

---

blocks.content: ### OAC — Origin Access Control (for S3)
### WAF + CloudFront

Attach WAF (Web Application Firewall) to CloudFront:
→ WAF rules run at the EDGE (400+ locations)
→ Malicious traffic blocked before reaching your origin
→ SQL injection, XSS, rate limiting, IP blocking — all at edge
→ CHEAPER: blocked requests don't consume your origin capacity

COMMON WAF RULES ON CLOUDFRONT:
→ AWS Managed Rules (OWASP Top 10 protection)
→ Rate limiting: >1000 requests/5min from same IP → block
→ Geo-blocking: block specific countries
→ IP reputation lists (known bad IPs from threat intelligence)
### HTTPS + Custom Certificates

CloudFront forces HTTPS with custom domain:
1. Request SSL certificate in ACM (us-east-1 region ONLY for CloudFront!)
2. Attach certificate to CloudFront distribution
3. Configure: redirect HTTP → HTTPS
4. Route 53 Alias: your domain → CloudFront distribution

SECURITY POLICIES:
TLSv1.2_2021 (recommended): TLS 1.2+ only, modern cipher suites
TLSv1 (legacy): if you must support very old clients

SNI (Server Name Indication): FREE (CloudFront uses SNI for certificate selection)
Dedicated IP: $600/month (for very old clients that don't support SNI)
→ Use SNI unless you have specific reason not to

revisionPoints: → CloudFront signs requests to S3 with a special signature
→ S3 bucket policy: only allow requests signed by CloudFront OAC
→ Direct S3 access → denied (no valid OAC signature)
→ Via CloudFront → allowed (has valid signature)
→ Block Public Access ON for S3 bucket (good!)
→ CloudFront OAC authenticates → gets objects
→ Users see content via CloudFront, never touch S3 directly
→ WAF rules run at the EDGE (400+ locations)

sections.title: ⏱️ Caching and TTL
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
HOW CACHING WORKS:
1. User requests file → nearest edge location
2. Edge checks cache → MISS (not cached or expired)
3. Edge fetches from origin → stores in cache with TTL
4. Next user for same file → edge cache HIT → serves immediately
5. TTL expires → next request → re-fetches from origin

TTL CONTROL (in order of priority):
1. Cache-Control: max-age=86400 (from your origin response)
2. Expires: [date] (from your origin response)
3. Default TTL in CloudFront behavior (if origin sends no cache headers)

DEFAULT TTL: 24 hours (86400 seconds)
MINIMUM TTL: 0 seconds
MAXIMUM TTL: 31,536,000 seconds (1 year)

BEST PRACTICES BY CONTENT TYPE:
/static/images/*    → max-age=31536000 (1 year) — use cache-busting filenames
/static/js/*.js     → max-age=31536000 — versioned: app.a1b2c3.js
/static/css/*.css   → max-age=31536000 — versioned
/api/user/profile   → max-age=0, no-cache (personalized, never cache)
/api/products       → max-age=300 (5 min — changes occasionally)
/                   → max-age=60 (index.html — update propagates in 1 min)

CACHE KEY (what makes a unique cache entry):
Default: URL path only
Can add: query strings, headers, cookies (increases cache misses but enables personalization)
→ Add query string to cache key: /products?category=shoes → different cache entry per category
→ Don't add unnecessary dimensions → lowers cache hit rate
```

### Cache Invalidation
```
PROBLEM: You deployed new code. CloudFront still serves old cached JS/CSS.

INVALIDATION:
→ Tell CloudFront to delete specific cached files
→ Cost: first 1000 invalidation paths/month FREE, then $0.005 per path
→ /* → invalidate everything (counts as 1 path)
→ /static/js/* → invalidate all JS files
→ Takes ~60 seconds to propagate to all edge locations

BETTER APPROACH — CACHE BUSTING:
→ Include file hash in filename: app.a1b2c3d4.js
→ Deploy new code → filename changes → app.e5f6g7h8.js
→ Old cache entry (a1b2c3d4) becomes irrelevant (nobody requests it)
→ New file → cache miss → fetched from origin → cached
→ HTML references new filename → users get new JS immediately
→ Zero invalidation cost. Works automatically with Webpack/Vite.

→ For index.html: short TTL (60s) or always invalidate on deploy
   (index.html can't have hash in its own name)
```

---

→ Don't add unnecessary dimensions → lowers cache hit rate

sections.title: 🌐 CloudFront Use Cases
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
1. STATIC WEBSITE (S3 + CloudFront):
   S3 bucket (static files) → CloudFront → Route 53 → users
   Cost: nearly zero for small sites
   Performance: files served from nearest edge location worldwide
   Security: OAC → S3 not public, HTTPS enforced

2. API ACCELERATION:
   API Gateway / ALB → CloudFront (for cacheable API responses)
   Even for dynamic APIs: CloudFront reduces TCP handshake time
   Uses AWS's optimized global network (faster than public internet routing)

3. VIDEO STREAMING:
   S3 or MediaStore → CloudFront → users
   HLS, DASH streaming at global scale
   Signed URLs/Cookies for access control (paid content)

4. LAMBDA@EDGE / CLOUDFRONT FUNCTIONS:
   Run code at edge locations (near users)
   Lambda@Edge: full Node.js, Python (max 5 seconds execution)
   CloudFront Functions: lightweight JS, < 1ms execution, much cheaper
   
   Use cases:
   → A/B testing at edge (no origin request needed)
   → Authentication: verify JWT at edge before forwarding to ALB
   → URL rewrites: /old-path → /new-path at edge
   → Add security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options) to every response
   → Personalization: insert user's country into request header for origin

5. DDoS PROTECTION (built-in):
   CloudFront + AWS Shield Standard → automatic DDoS mitigation at edge
   Absorbs attack traffic before it reaches your origin
   Scale: CloudFront can absorb much larger attacks than your origin could
```

---

blocks.content:
1. STATIC WEBSITE (S3 + CloudFront):
   S3 bucket (static files) → CloudFront → Route 53 → users
   Cost: nearly zero for small sites
   Performance: files served from nearest edge location worldwide
   Security: OAC → S3 not public, HTTPS enforced

2. API ACCELERATION:
   API Gateway / ALB → CloudFront (for cacheable API responses)
   Even for dynamic APIs: CloudFront reduces TCP handshake time
   Uses AWS's optimized global network (faster than public internet routing)

3. VIDEO STREAMING:
   S3 or MediaStore → CloudFront → users
   HLS, DASH streaming at global scale
   Signed URLs/Cookies for access control (paid content)

4. LAMBDA@EDGE / CLOUDFRONT FUNCTIONS:
   Run code at edge locations (near users)
   Lambda@Edge: full Node.js, Python (max 5 seconds execution)
   CloudFront Functions: lightweight JS, < 1ms execution, much cheaper
   
   Use cases:
   → A/B testing at edge (no origin request needed)
   → Authentication: verify JWT at edge before forwarding to ALB
   → URL rewrites: /old-path → /new-path at edge
   → Add security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options) to every response
   → Personalization: insert user's country into request header for origin

5. DDoS PROTECTION (built-in):
   CloudFront + AWS Shield Standard → automatic DDoS mitigation at edge
   Absorbs attack traffic before it reaches your origin
   Scale: CloudFront can absorb much larger attacks than your origin could

revisionPoints: S3 bucket (static files) → CloudFront → Route 53 → users

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — CloudFront
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: Why use CloudFront in front of S3 instead of serving directly from S3?**
```
"Four reasons:

Performance: S3 is regional (one location). CloudFront has 400+ edge
locations. User in Tokyo gets files from Tokyo edge, not your us-east-1
bucket. Latency drops from 180ms to 5ms for cached content.

Cost: S3 to internet data transfer is $0.09/GB. CloudFront to internet
is $0.0085/GB (90% cheaper) because CloudFront has preferential rates with
ISPs and optimized transfer from S3 to CloudFront is also cheaper.

Security: with OAC, S3 bucket is completely private. Block Public Access
is ON. Only CloudFront can read objects. Direct S3 URLs fail. Users always
go through CloudFront (WAF, rate limiting, geo-blocking all apply).

HTTPS: S3 static website hosting doesn't support HTTPS with custom domain.
CloudFront + ACM certificate = HTTPS with your domain. Required for SEO
and browser trust.

The combination is: Route 53 → CloudFront (HTTPS, WAF, CDN) → S3 (OAC,
private, origin). This is the standard pattern for all static sites."
```

**Q: How do you ensure users always get the latest version of your SPA after deployment?**
```
"Two-part strategy:

Part 1: Cache-bust JS/CSS with content hashes.
Modern build tools (Vite, webpack) append file content hash to filename:
main.a1b2c3d4.js, styles.e5f6g7h8.css
CloudFront caches these with max-age=31536000 (1 year).
When code changes → new hash → new filename → new cache entry.
Old cached files are never requested again (dead cache entries harmless).

Part 2: Short TTL on index.html.
index.html references the hashed filenames.
Set: Cache-Control: max-age=60, must-revalidate on index.html responses.
Or: invalidate /index.html from CloudFront on every deployment.

Result: users cache JS/CSS for 1 year (fast!), always get fresh index.html
within 60 seconds of deployment. index.html references new hashed files.
New hashes → cache miss → fetched from origin → cached at edge.
Total update propagation: < 2 minutes after deployment. No manual invalidation."
```

**Q: Users in one region still see stale content while others see the new site. How do you debug CloudFront?**
```
"I would think cache key and cache lifetime first.

Checklist:
→ Is the stale object actually cached at edge because TTL is still valid?
→ Did we invalidate the right path, especially index.html?
→ Are query strings, headers, or cookies part of cache key unexpectedly?
→ Is origin returning different cache-control in different paths?
→ Is CloudFront even serving from cache, or are we seeing origin inconsistency?

I would compare response headers:
→ x-cache
→ age
→ cache-control
→ etag / last-modified

The senior lesson is that stale content is often not 'CloudFront is broken';
it is usually a mismatch between cache strategy and deployment strategy."
```

**Q: When can CloudFront make an outage worse instead of better?**
```
"If the cache policy is wrong, CloudFront can amplify bad behavior globally.

Examples:
→ cache a bad HTML response and every region serves it
→ forward too many headers/cookies and destroy cache hit ratio
→ no origin shielding and massive cache miss storm hits origin after deploy
→ dynamic API responses cached incorrectly and users see other users' data

So CloudFront is not just a speed layer. It is a distribution layer.
Any mistake at that layer becomes global very quickly.

That is why I treat cache policy, origin failover, and invalidation strategy as production controls, not CDN details."
```

---

→ Did we invalidate the right path, especially index.html?
→ Are query strings, headers, or cookies part of cache key unexpectedly?
→ Is origin returning different cache-control in different paths?
→ Is CloudFront even serving from cache, or are we seeing origin inconsistency?
→ x-cache
→ age
→ cache-control

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CloudFront ← S3: static files origin (with OAC)
CloudFront ← ALB: dynamic app origin
CloudFront ← API Gateway: API endpoint origin
CloudFront ← Custom origin: any HTTP server
CloudFront → WAF: attach for request filtering
CloudFront → ACM: SSL certificates (must be in us-east-1)
CloudFront → Route 53: Alias record pointing to distribution
CloudFront → Lambda@Edge: run code at edge
CloudFront → CloudFront Functions: lightweight edge logic
CloudFront → S3 Access Logs: request logging

NEXT: SQS, SNS, EventBridge — Messaging & Integration
How your services communicate asynchronously and decouple from each other.
```

---
---
